title: Open Workshop: Delegation, Pricing, Scaling, and Business Model Clarity
summary: Participants brought live challenges including a flooding project crisis while the owner was out of town, questions about how to pursue higher-value clients, and pricing structure comparisons. Erin and group members discussed the necessity of stepping back to let project managers grow, how character and systems win clients at higher price points, and the importance of knowing what kind of business you actually want to build.

teachings.concept: Delegation as a Prerequisite for Scaling
teachings.explanation: Business owners cannot grow their companies without learning to step away and let their team handle problems. Even imperfect execution by a project manager establishes a new baseline for the company and reveals the team's true capabilities. Holding on too tightly prevents growth.
teachings.quote: If anyone here wants to scale you have to learn to step away period or you'll never grow. If we're holding their hand the whole time... you're going to learn something about yourself and them and how to improve the systems.

teachings.explanation: When delegating, owners should accept that a project manager getting 80% of the way there is a win, not a failure. That 80% becomes the new baseline from which the company can improve. Expecting perfection prevents delegation.
teachings.quote: Back off and let them get 80 there. He's not going to say everything perfect we all know that but if he gets you 80 there then you now have a new baseline for your company.

teachings.concept: Character and Systems as the True Sales Pitch
teachings.explanation: Clients at higher price points are not primarily buying technical skill — they are buying the confidence that someone will take care of them. Demonstrating systems, process, and trustworthiness wins work more reliably than portfolio or credentials alone.
teachings.quote: People hire character and I'm proof. The only thing I could lean on was doing what I said I would do.

teachings.concept: Business Model Clarity Based on Lifestyle Goals
teachings.explanation: There is no single right answer for how big a business should grow. Some owners build intentionally small, lifestyle-centered businesses and that is equally valid. The key is being clear about what you actually want before deciding how to structure and grow your company.
teachings.quote: She's modeled this business based around her lifestyle. Her revenue goals are not as aggressive as what mine might be. Does that make sense?

teachings.concept: Process as the Sales Tool for High-Value Clients
teachings.explanation: When clients ask about finances or money management, the right answer is to describe your draw process in detail. Walking a prospective client through how invoices are reviewed, cross-checked, and approved answers their underlying concerns about being taken care of without requiring financial disclosure.
teachings.quote: That homeowner feels super super super relieved and all concern is gone if all of these people are saying hey this is okay to pay. It's explained in a process and to me that's the sell.

teachings.explanation: Educating clients about the difference between quality-focused builders and volume/investor builders is a legitimate and important part of the sales process. Clients cannot make informed decisions without understanding that low fees often correlate with trade substitution, change orders, or quality shortcuts.
teachings.quote: If you want that price this is the business model that's how it looks. But if you want quality this is the business model. They're different. And people don't know that — why would they know it?

teachings.concept: Flexibility in Getting to the Next Level
teachings.explanation: Moving from remodeling to new construction or from smaller to larger projects sometimes requires flexibility in how you structure fees or contracts to get that first key project. Being open to creative contract structures can be the bridge to a new tier of work, with the opportunity to renegotiate as scope expands.
teachings.quote: I knew it was a step in the right direction. Halfway through he says oh my gosh I want you to handle all the landscaping and the pool. And I said great but I would like to restructure this contract. And that was my chance.

teachings.concept: Higher Percentage Fees on Smaller Projects
teachings.explanation: Smaller renovation projects should carry a higher percentage markup than large new construction, not lower. The overhead, complexity, and risk per dollar of construction cost is higher on small jobs, and builders should not undercharge simply because the total dollar amount is lower.
teachings.quote: The smaller the project the higher percentages that you should be making on the job.

actionable_advice: When traveling or unavailable, explicitly empower your project manager to make decisions with boots on the ground rather than trying to manage remotely through photos and video calls.
Before leaving town, establish clear communication protocols with your team: what is being covered, when, and how updates will be reported.
When a project stalls on budget disagreements, ask the client directly 'What makes sense to you?' and then stop talking — let them propose a structure and use that as a negotiation starting point.

If a client asks about your financials or capacity, redirect to your draw process and sub payment history rather than disclosing financial statements. Offer to have them call your top vendors' accounts receivable to verify payment track record.
When facing retainage demands from residential clients, explain that trades work week to week and retainage is a commercial concept that does not apply to residential construction.

Share stories of how your trade relationships deliver value beyond the contract — for example, helping a friend or colleague quickly — to illustrate relationship capital when speaking with prospective clients.

Charge a pre-construction fee for estimating and design work. This can be rolled into the construction fee if they proceed, but it filters serious clients and ensures you are compensated for your time.
Do not discount your design fees. If clients ask why they should pay for design, ask them directly why anyone would work for free — put the burden on them to justify not paying.
On smaller renovation projects under $500K, charge a higher percentage markup than you would on larger projects to account for proportionally higher overhead and complexity.
Consider doing exploratory wall openings and site investigation before finalizing a fixed-price contract on renovations to eliminate surprises and protect your margin.
client_challenges: Denise's project flooded overnight while she was out of state caring for her mother after surgery, and her relatively new project manager had to handle the crisis without her direct involvement.

Randall struggles with self-doubt when considering bidding on projects significantly larger than his current highest-value build, questioning whether he has the credentials and financial standing to compete for 5-10 million dollar homes.
Randall repeatedly loses bids to lower-priced competitors even when clients express strong preference for him, especially on projects under $5 million where budget sensitivity is acute.

Tiffany is uncertain about her growth direction and whether she wants to pursue larger, more demanding projects given that high-budget clients expect constant availability and she values flexibility and mental breaks.

Multiple participants are unsure how to structure their fees — builder fee, project management fee, and design fee — in a way that is competitive but actually profitable, especially after years of undercharging.
Participants are concerned about how to respond when prospective high-end clients ask about company financials or capacity to handle a significantly larger project than previously completed.
Sandra noted she pauses all projects when traveling because she is a one-person operation, highlighting the structural challenge of being the sole point of contact on every project.
notable_quotes: On stepping away to grow: 'I'm not that important. I'm really not that important in this situation. And it's true — they figure it out.'
On what wins high-end clients: 'People hire character. Building is not rocket science. Here's what we're going to do — we're focused on you and your goals.'
On persistence over brilliance: 'The people who do really well in life are not super bright. They're not brilliant. They're persistent and they're clear.'

On low-fee builders: 'If you're charging nine to ten percent, typically what's going on is that a builder gets bids and uses the cheaper trade and makes a profit on that delta. I could not be in business at nine or ten percent.'
On charging for design work: 'Why would anyone want someone to work for free? It doesn't even make sense. You can throw it back at people — why would I work for free?'

On knowing your business model: 'I have plenty of friends that have wonderful businesses that they love and they don't have any interest in getting into all these other problems that we invite ourselves to.'
